facts.size: About 11–12 cm — smaller than a sparrow
facts.habitat: Deciduous woodland, parks, hedgerows, and gardens
facts.migration: Mostly resident; some cold-weather wandering in hard winters

hints.region: One of the most common garden birds across Europe and western Asia — the most frequent visitor to garden feeders in Britain
hints.size: Tiny — about 11–12 cm, lighter than a 10-pence coin, and extremely acrobatic
hints.behavior: Hangs upside-down to feed on caterpillars in tree canopies; pairs return to the same nest box every year and can have over a dozen chicks in a single clutch

funFact: Blue Tit clutch size is precisely timed to peak caterpillar abundance — pairs that start even a week too early or late have significantly fewer surviving chicks.

facts.size: About 24–25 cm — similar to an American Robin
facts.migration: Largely resident; northern populations move south in winter

hints.region: One of the most widespread and familiar birds across Europe, from Scandinavia to North Africa and east into Asia
hints.size: About 25 cm — similar in size to an American Robin, but slimmer and longer-tailed
hints.behavior: Males sing a rich, melodious song from rooftops at dawn and dusk — one of the most celebrated bird songs in Europe; runs across lawns stopping to listen for worms

funFact: The Common Blackbird is a relative newcomer to suburban habitats — it was primarily a woodland bird until the 19th century, when it rapidly colonised European cities.

facts.habitat: Open farmland, meadows, and marshes near villages and towns
facts.diet: Frogs, insects, small mammals, reptiles, and fish
facts.migration: Long-distance migrant — breeds in Europe, winters in sub-Saharan Africa

hints.region: Breeds across Europe from Iberia to central Asia; winters in Africa south of the Sahara — a long-distance migrant covering up to 20,000 km each year
hints.size: A large, unmistakable wading bird — up to 115 cm tall with a wingspan of over 2 metres
hints.behavior: Nests on rooftops, chimneys, and purpose-built platforms in villages; pairs reunite at the nest each spring and perform a greeting ceremony of loud bill-clattering

funFact: White Storks are completely silent — they have no song or call muscles. Their only vocalisation is a bill-clattering display at the nest.

facts.size: About 14 cm — slightly larger than a Blue Tit
facts.habitat: Deciduous and mixed woodland, parks, and gardens
facts.migration: Mostly resident; some dispersal in cold winters

hints.region: The most widespread tit in the world — found from western Europe across Asia to Japan; one of the most-studied birds in ornithology
hints.size: About 14 cm — slightly larger than a House Sparrow, with a striking black-and-white head pattern and bright yellow belly
hints.behavior: Has one of the largest and most variable song repertoires of any bird — can learn new songs by listening to neighbours and has been recorded using over 40 different calls

funFact: Great Tit populations in urban areas have evolved slightly longer bills compared to rural birds, thought to be an adaptation to bird feeder feeding over just a few decades.

facts.size: About 34–35 cm — similar to a Jackdaw
facts.habitat: Oak woodland, mixed forest, parks, and large gardens
facts.diet: Acorns (primary), insects, eggs, nestlings, and berries
facts.migration: Mostly resident; large irruptions in years of acorn failure

hints.region: Found across Europe and Asia from Britain to Japan — shy and often heard before seen in oak woodland
hints.size: About 35 cm — larger than a Blackbird, with a streaked pinkish-brown body and striking blue wing patches
hints.behavior: Buries thousands of acorns each autumn as a food store and can remember the location of hundreds of individual caches; many forgotten caches germinate into oak trees

funFact: Eurasian Jays have been shown to 'plan for the future' by caching food based on what they expect to need tomorrow — a form of mental time travel previously thought unique to humans.
